results.possibleIdentity: Grace Bakst Wapner (1934–2025) was an American sculptor and installation artist who was prominently active beginning in the late 1960s. She is known for her diverse use of materials, including Styrofoam, polyurethane, fabric, and ceramics. In the 1960s, she shared a studio in Manhattan with the influential artist Eva Hesse and was part of a circle of artists that included Ethelyn Honig, Jackie Winsor, and Barbara Zucker. Her early work focused on geological and biomorphic forms made from carved and painted Styrofoam and polyurethane. Wapner was a founding member of the 55 Mercer Street Gallery (an artist-run cooperative in New York) and was included in the landmark 1971 exhibition 'Twenty Six Contemporary Women Artists' curated by Lucy Lippard at the Aldrich Museum of Contemporary Art. In the late 1960s, she was associated with the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, as evidenced by her inclusion in the E.A.T. newsletters and correspondence within the Gerd Stern papers. results.possibleIdentity: Leah Nolan (1901–1982), also known as Leah Williams Nolan, was an American artist and illustrator whose career spanned painting, figure construction, and early video art. She was a notable participant in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) movement during the late 1960s, contributing to the organization's collaborative efforts between artists and engineers. Her work was featured in the significant E.A.T. exhibition 'Some More Beginnings' at the Brooklyn Museum (1968–1969). Earlier in her career, she moved to Chicago in 1933 where she created miniature figures for cartoon illustrations in magazines such as Esquire, Look, and the Saturday Evening Post. She later studied painting at the School of Design in Chicago under the Hungarian artist László Moholy-Nagy. By 1950, she moved to New York City, alternating between a career in commercial drafting and fine art painting. Her artistic output included a series of five paintings titled 'Aggregate,' and she continued to experiment with new techniques and media, including video, until her later years. She eventually returned to Kansas, where she died in 1982.

After studying at Cooper Union and Black Mountain College—where he met influential figures like John Cage, Merce Cunningham, and Buckminster Fuller—VanDerBeek began creating experimental collage films such as 'Breathdeath' (1963). In the mid-1960s, he built the 'Movie Drome' in Stony Point, New York, a grain-silo theater designed for immersive, multi-projector experiences. VanDerBeek was a frequent collaborator with engineers and scientists, most notably working with Ken Knowlton at Bell Telephone Laboratories to produce the 'Poem Field' series (1964–1968), which were some of the earliest examples of computer-animated art. His work was deeply aligned with the mission of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), as he advocated for a 'utopian fusion' of art and technology to create a new global visual language. Throughout the late 1960s and 1970s, he held residencies at institutions such as MIT's Center for Advanced Visual Studies (CAVS), NASA, and WGBH-TV, where he experimented with fax murals, interactive television ('Violence Sonata'), and steam-screen projections.

results.possibleIdentity: Gary Allen is an artist, illustrator, and photographer who was active during the late 1960s and 1970s, particularly within the artistic community known as the "Golden Age of New Paltz" (associated with SUNY New Paltz). He was a member of Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), an organization that promoted collaborations between artists and engineers. During this period, he collaborated with professor Robert Schuler on projects involving experimental buildings constructed from sprayed urethane foam. Allen's artistic practice often integrated scientific and mathematical principles, such as using mathematics to determine color sequences for paintings and designing specialized lighting systems. He also practiced postal art. In his later career, Allen transitioned into writing and food history, authoring or editing approximately 40 books on the subject, including "The Herbalist in the Kitchen" and "Sauces Reconsidered."

results.possibleIdentity: Eve Peret (historically identified as Eve Peretz; born 1934) is an American multimedia artist who was active in the New York art scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. She is closely associated with the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), having joined the group shortly after its founding. A graduate of The Cooper Union (Class of 1965), she became known for her experimental sculptures that integrated industrial materials with technology. In 1968, through E.A.T.'s matching service, she collaborated with engineer L. J. Sliker from Bell Laboratories to develop electronic control systems and variable intensity light sources for her sculptures. Her work was featured in several landmark exhibitions of the era, most notably the E.A.T.-organized exhibition 'Some More Beginnings' at the Brooklyn Museum in 1968 and the 'Annual Exhibition of Contemporary American Sculpture' at the Whitney Museum of American Art in 1970. Her sculptures, often constructed from materials such as polyester resin, fiberglass, and light, were also exhibited at the O.K. Harris Gallery in SoHo. Her work remains in the permanent collection of the Whitney Museum of American Art. She continued to exhibit throughout the 1970s and 1980s at venues including the SoHo Center for Visual Artists and A.I.R. Gallery.
